posture: Watch — one fresh, dated NSW-REZ-relevant development cleared the relevance bar in the 24 hours to Tuesday morning: Squadron Energy has unveiled a new 558MW wind project, Banandra, on grazing land south-east of Darlington Point and just east of the declared South West REZ, and — because the South West REZ has already reached capacity — says it will seek to plug into the existing 330kV Darlington Point-to-Wagga Wagga line rather than pursue REZ access rights. It is the latest concrete illustration of the recurring South West REZ capacity/voltage constraint (the zone is rated at 330kV via Project EnergyConnect rather than the 500kV many developers wanted, and has granted access rights to only four projects, three of them scaled down), now pushing new capacity to bypass the REZ and connect to the existing network. It is very early-stage — community consultation began in May, with the state planning application and EPBC referral lodged at the start of July — so it is logged as situational-awareness 'inform'. Everything heavier is a prior-digest carry-forward: the Draft New England REZ Access Scheme Declaration remains on public exhibition (released 7 July, feedback by 6 August, targeted end-September ministerial declaration), with EnergyCo's industry and community webinars taking place today, Tuesday 14 July; the New England corridor field-investigation program runs July-September across the refined 1km Muswellbrook-Walcha corridor to Bayswater (~150 affected landholders) amid live Walcha High Country Guardians opposition, the Coalition's late-June policy to scrap the 500kV corridor and cap the zone into the March 2027 election run-up, and a community-group alliance's threat to disengage from consultation; the Central-West Orana Port-to-REZ OSOM delivery campaign to Squadron's Uungula Wind Farm that began Monday 13 July (with the new Main Road No. 709 gazettal around Muswellbrook) continues; and on Central-West Orana, ACEREZ's late-June recommencement of access-track work under stronger controls remains the latest word while the DPHI rock-shelter heritage assessment stays open.
summary: A quiet scan surfaced one fresh NSW-REZ-relevant development: Squadron Energy has unveiled the 558MW Banandra wind project on grazing land just east of the at-capacity South West REZ, and says it will seek to connect to the existing 330kV Darlington Point-Wagga Wagga line rather than seek REZ access rights. The project is at its earliest stages (consultation from May; state planning application and EPBC referral lodged early July, flagging a worst-case impact on up to 35 threatened species), and it re-illustrates the South West REZ capacity and voltage constraint that is pushing new generation to bypass the zone; it is logged as 'inform'. All other threads — the New England Access Scheme consultation window and its 14 July webinars, the July-September field-investigation program, the Walcha-area and Coalition opposition and the alliance's disengagement threat, the Central-West Orana OSOM delivery campaign and Main Road No. 709 gazettal, and the ACEREZ rock-shelter matter — are prior-digest entries with no fresh movement in the window and are deduplicated out per the writing rules.

items.headline: Squadron unveils a 558MW wind project beside the at-capacity South West REZ, planning to bypass it and plug into the existing 330kV network
items.summary: Squadron Energy has announced the proposed 558MW Banandra wind project, a 90-turbine development across about 11,600 hectares of mostly grazing land south-east of Darlington Point, immediately east of the declared South West REZ. Because the South West REZ has already reached capacity, Squadron says Banandra will seek to connect into the existing 330kV line between Darlington Point and Wagga Wagga rather than pursue REZ access rights, mirroring other developers shut out of a zone that ended up rated at 330kV (via Project EnergyConnect) rather than the 500kV many had wanted; the REZ has granted access rights to only four projects, three of them scaled down. The project is very early-stage, with community consultation from May and the state planning application and EPBC referral lodged at the start of July, the referral flagging a worst-case impact on up to 35 threatened species pending field surveys.
items.so_what: Banandra is a fresh, concrete marker of the structural South West REZ capacity and voltage constraint that keeps surfacing on the radar: rather than adding to the zone, a major developer is routing around it onto the existing network, which underscores questions about whether the REZ as designed can absorb the generation queue and hints at growing uncoordinated connection pressure on nearby shared infrastructure. For EnergyCo the signal is one of REZ design adequacy and market-confidence risk — repeated bypass strategies erode the case for the zone's transmission investment and can complicate system planning — while the early EPBC/threatened-species flag is an environmental-approval and cumulative-impact thread to watch as the South West pipeline builds out.
